narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 30.50, and solid Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 5th in your school—both slightly below the typical student's standing. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 50.9% chance that you would fail the bar, tipping the scales just over the threshold for rejection. This close call meant your application was denied, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass confidently. The most influential factor pulling in your favor was your Year 3 decile ranking, which suggested stronger relative performance later in school and nudged the model toward expecting bar exam success. However, your LSAT score had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as it fell notably below the average of around 36.7 seen in successful students. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, also lower than the typical 3.21, further reinforcing the model's concerns about bar passage risk. Your family income bracket contributed somewhat to the failure prediction, perhaps reflecting patterns in the data where it correlates with outcomes, while your Year 1 decile had a milder negative influence. Planning to attend full-time was a small positive, aligning with the vast majority of students who succeed. Compared to typical applicants, your academics are competitive but not standout, especially on standardized tests where higher scores dominate bar success stories. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above average, building on your improving Year 3 performance, and highlighting any additional experiences that demonstrate resilience. We're rooting for you—many students in similar positions turn things around with targeted improvements. Keep pursuing your legal ambitions; your story isn't over yet.
